replies.title: Re: Counter-Intuitive Outcomes
replies.content: No, I don't have a book to recommend, but I do have advice. Strenuous exercise doesn't do your lifespan any good, quite the contrary -- in Chinese martial arts circles, the wai-chia (hard style) methods are considered great for young men but not at all for middle-aged and older ones. Gentle exercise with a strong etheric component is the standard recommendation once you're past youth. comments.title: Aquarian Music
comments.content: Based on what you’ve mentioned about the Age of Aquarius before— started in the late 19th century, will usher in an age of idiosyncrasy (if I’m distilling correctly)— then might jazz have possibly been the first Aquarian musical movement? replies.title: Re: Aquarian Music
replies.content: The classic ethos in jazz only worked because there was still a lot of unexplored space within the jazz idiom; by the 1960s that was mined out, and since then -- like any other mature art form -- jazz has been original or it's been good, but almost never both.  I see jazz and rap both as foreshocks of the Aquarian energy, not yet fully embodying it, but beginning to catch some of its possibilities while still remaining within a basically Piscean framework.

replies.title: Re: Lovers Tarot Card
replies.content: Go on to other cards. Notice when they have patterns that echo the Lovers -- for example, the two figures standing before the Hierophant and the Devil echo the position of the figures of the Lovers. Also, go back over your divinations and see if you can figure out what events they were talking about -- that will give you another way to see the meaning of each card in action.

replies.title: Re: Lovers Tarot Card
replies.content: Would it help to go back to the Marseilles pack? The Lovers is one card which is very different in the Rider-Waite.

replies.title: Re: Lovers Tarot Card
replies.content: No, keep working with your existing deck. Each deck is like a different language, and you're not likely to figure out a word in French by looking it up in a dictionary of Russian!

replies.title: Re: Lovers Tarot Card
replies.content: The Marseilles question wasn't from the OP, sorry, I should have made that clearer. But now you mention it, R-W clearly derives from Marseilles, and Waite must have had a reason, one presumes, to completely change that card in particular. After all, he didn't decide to start from scratch and create a completely new deck, rather, he decided to start with an existing deck and tweak it to his liking, so I'm not sure if I agree that the two are completely different languages. So is it not valid in this case to go back a step and look at the originals, especially if, as in this case, the meaning of the original is somewhat easier to discern?

replies.title: Re: Lovers Tarot Card
replies.content: Only if you're doing it in the context of a more general study of the origins of tarot imagery. That's equivalent to etymology, which is not the same thing as knowing what a word means now. (The English word "black" used to mean "white" -- it's cognate with French blanc.)

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Can the text and images of Splendor Solis be used for practices like discursive meditation and scrying? If so, does that require some prior knowledge of alchemical terminology?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Yes and yes. Splendor Solis is an intermediate alchemical text -- it's far less difficult than some (say, Fulcanelli's The Mystery of the Cathedrals) but it's not a beginner's textbook. Start by reading a couple of modern introductions -- The Alchemists Handbook by Frater Albertus and The Path of Alchemy by Mark Stavish would be good choices -- and then read some of the standard basic works such as The Book of Lambspring and The Chemical Wedding of Christian Rosenkreutz. (Don't expect to understand either of these at first glance, or at a hundred and first, but they'll get you used to the symbolism.) Then go on to Splendor Solis and see what you can make of it.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: If I may make a suggestion, Adam McLean provides some courses on alchemical symbols, emblems, and texts - lessons in their interpretation well-suited to discursive meditation - available here: https://www.alchemywebsite.com/bookshop/study_course.html

Dear JMG,

Did you choose "compassion" instead of "empathy" for any particular reason? Back in college, I was taught (in some workshops) that empathy is really the deeper one b/c you're feeling what the other feels where as compassion is feeling what you *would feel if you were in their shoes*. A quick internet search shows me that the debate rages on of what's "better".

In this case, did you suggest "compassion" to avoid mixing on the etheric level?
Did you intentionally avoid using "empathy"?
Is it risker to use "empathy" vs "compassion" to your mind?

Thanks again!
Etheric Inquiring

replies.title: Re: Affirmation: Compassion vs Empathy
replies.content: Empathy involves physically and emotionally experiencing their pain, which is not useful unless you need drama in your life. Compassion involves being aware of their pain but not caught up in it directly, so that you can take intelligent action in response to it without having your mind and emotions overwhelmed by reactions to pain. Which one is "better" is a useless discussion unless "better for what purpose?" is asked and answered. If you're going to help another person, it doesn't help you to be doubled over, moaning and sobbing, unable to think clearly!

replies.title: Re: Affirmation follow-up

The next step is simply to assess your experience with the affirmation and decide what you want to take on next. You can continue working with that affirmation if you like, of course, or work out another one. My point was simply that work with affirmations is something that takes a while!

replies.title: Re: more on abstraction

comments.title: When to keep your mouth shut
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

As always, thank you again for these Magic Mondays!

Occasionally I’ve caught note of people mentioning there are certain things that one should not talk about or discuss regarding magical or occult practices, not just for common sense reasons of when it’s wise to keep your mouth shut, but also because it has some impact or effect on the magical or occult practice or experience itself. For example, I think remember reading that it’s best not to talk about spiritual experiences you’ve had personally.

Without asking you to cross any of those lines and compromise anything in your life or the lives of others by talking about it openly, would you be willing to talk a bit about this general concept? What kinds of magical/spiritual/occult things I should just keep my mouth shut about for magical reasons, and if possible the influences/reasons that make this the case?

Also, any advice on when to keep my mouth shut for purely mundane and practical reasons that you feel is worth mentioning would also be welcome here.

Much gratitude and many thanks, and I hope life is going very well in your corner these days.

replies.title: Re: When to keep your mouth shut
replies.content:
Sure. Let's start with the basics. If you're playing a poker game, and you let the other fellows know what cards you have in your hand, you lose. That applies to every situation where different people seek different goals; if the other people know what you have in mind, they can mess with you, and a good many of them will. That's one very broad category of things where you want to keep your mouth shut.

Second, have you ever been around the kind of wannabee writer who is always talking about the books he wants to write, laying them out in boring detail to anyone who will listen? Such people never actually finish those books, and for good reason: the creative energy goes into jabber, rather than writing. This is true of all creative activity, and defines a second category.

Third, if you knew that the building down the block was full of buckets of gasoline, and the neighborhood was full of teenage arsonists, would you talk about those buckets of gasoline? I hope not. That defines a third category.

Those three categories -- situations of competition in which talking about your resources and plans is a good way to lose; situations of creativity in which talking about your intentions disperses the creative tension and energy; and situations of danger where talking about what you know could give the clueless and the malicious ways to cause far more damage than they otherwise would -- sum up the practical dimensions of magical secrecy. There's another dimension, which is the way that secrecy can be used to train the will and develop certain inner capacities; I've discussed those in detail in a chapter of my book Inside a Magical Lodge.

replies.title: Re: Reincarnation sequence
replies.content: The mental sheath is one of several parts of the human that survives between lives. You have the mental sheath, and then you also have the soul -- the eternal part of the individual, which exists on the three planes above the mental plane, but is still unformed and unevolved (we haven't started working on those planes yet).  So you have a soul with a mental sheath, and you have a fetus -- a physical body and an etheric body.  At quickening, the soul descends from the mental plane (where it's been embodied in its mental sheath) through the astral plane, where it gathers up a mass of unformed astral substance, to the fetus. The astral substance provides the link between the soul-plus-mental-sheath, on the one hand, and the physical and etheric bodies, on the other; that link gradually strengthens over the weeks that follow, until the soul, mental sheath, and a newly formed astral body are integrated with the etheric and material bodies.

comments.title: Occult power of Temples
comments.content:
Some years ago I read The Secret of the Temple. I had to get it interlibrary loan, so I don't have it in front of me. But I remember you said there was a special quality of the stone they used to build cathedrals.

replies.title: Re: Occult power of Temples
replies.content:
The quality in question is paramagnetism -- a property of some material substances that aren't magnetic, but are weakly attracted by magnetic fields and generate internal magnetic fields in response. Some forms of stone are more paramagnetic than others, and it's an interesting fact that (for example) the old stone circles of northwestern Europe, and many old cathedrals, are made of unusually paramagnetic stone. It's not that there's one variety of stone that does it -- even within a type of stone (say, sandstone) some outcrops are more paramagnetic than others.

As for brick, I don't recall any discussion of that in the sources. Paramagnetism is very common in materials that have a lot of iron in them, though, so red brick (made of iron-rich clay) might well be paramagnetic enough to do the job.

replies.content:

comments.title: Vine Deloria Jr.
comments.content:
Hello Mr. Greer,

A couple months or so ago you published an article on Vine Deloria Jr.'s "The World We Used to Live In." In that article you mention that in the past magic was allegedly much stronger, even able to directly affect the physical world. The article got me thinking about possible explanations as to why this is so (assuming we can trust the ancient accounts as at least partially accurate). I've come up with a few possible options and am curious to know if you have put additional thought into this subject.

First, magic might grow stronger as the people in an area collectively develop a relationship with that region. This would work with reincarnation. Suppose tribe A lives in valley B for 5,000 years. During that time someone might be reincarnated there over and over. Thus, they would have many lifetimes to develop a relationship with the conscious entities in the area and thus become incredible mages. This would assume that reincarnation was following a particular tribe and region, but I think this works with what I know of Native American and Australian Aborigine's religion. But even without reincarnation, it seems like a spirit might trust you more if you ancestors have repeatedly been respectful towards it.

Second, magic might ebb and flow with the astrological cycles. I know very little about this, but I know you are a very competent astrologer, so I am interested to know if you find that certain points in the astrology cycles are high or low points in the power of magic on the kind of scale we are talking about here.

Third, magic might be the ying to the science yang. Maybe as we develop technology we distance ourselves from the power of magic, and vice versa. If this is so it begs the question of how a civilization would get the best of both worlds. I know the Egyptians seem to have pulled it off, as well as long lasting oriental cultures, but it seems like only civilizations that don't burn out quickly can do so. Thus, your civilization has to keep its technological progress within reasonable limits for it to last long enough for magical traditions to build, right? replies.title: Re: Entities

comments.title: Languages
comments.content:
Hi JMG, thanks for the weekly opportunity to ask some questions. My question is this: I have a decent background in Arabic and Latin (among other languages) from my academic training, but most of what I learned has grown rusty with age. I am considering getting back into studying some languages so I can read otherwise untranslated documents, particularly those related to magic. replies.title: Re: Languages
replies.content:
Arabic and Latin are two of the most valuable languages you could possibly learn for that purpose, as there are vast numbers of magical writings in both languages that have never been translated into English -- more, in fact, than any other language from the western half of Eurasia. Koine Greek, Persian, German, French, and Russian are all well stocked with magical texts, too -- they're the next rank down. In terms of Eastern languages -- dear gods, how could you choose? Sanskrit and Chinese are probably the most overwhelmingly supplied with untranslated magical texts, but Tibetan, Tamil, Thai, Japanese -- the list goes on and on.

More generally, pick a language, any language, and you've got your choice of vast amounts of untranslated magical texts waiting for you.

replies.title: Re: Languages
replies.content: I wasn't even thinking of eastern languages when I wrote my question, but that consideration certainly does expand the range of possibilities!  The other languages you mentioned all make sense to me, but I know very little about Russian magical traditions.  Are these Russian magical texts mostly rooted in the broader European Renaissance magical tradition, or are they centered in particularly Russian folkways?  Or central Asian traditions, or Caucasian traditions, or something else I haven't even considered, perhaps?

replies.title: Re: Languages
replies.content: The Russian texts I know of are rooted in European traditions -- there's a very large and rich current of Russian Martinism, for example -- but it wouldn't surprise me for a moment if there were also Caucasian and central Asian traditions, as well as old Slavic Pagan material, rattling around there too. Russia is a big country (how's that for an understatement?) and it's got room for a vast amount of magic.

replies.title: Re: Languages
replies.content: IIRC, Papus spent some time in Russia among the esoteric Freemasons there (most of whom were Grand Orient rather than Grand Lodge).

replies.title: Re: Languages
replies.content: I recall reading the same thing. That's where the Martinist current in Russian occultism got going -- Papus was the founder of the modern Martinist Order.

replies.title: Re: Languages
replies.content:
For older Russian magical texts there is now a superb reference work in English, Wiliam F. Ryan's "The Bathhouse at Midnight" (1999). (Ryan was the librarian at the Warburg Institute in London, the library of which has superb holdings of older occult texts, both published and unpublished. The editions of the Picatrix in Arabic and in Latin were the work of Warburg scholars.) Older Russian magical texts owe as much or more to Byzantine learned magic as to Western European learned magic; and Russian folkloric magic is something else again. The language of these texts is usually not modern Russian at all, but Old Slavonic, which is almost unintelligible to a Westerner who knows only a bit of late 20th-century Russian. (Native Russians have much less trouble getting the gist of these old texts, but they will have had a much broader exposure to all sorts of Russian.)

Before Ryan's book appeared I had written a short survey article on the subject, "Magic in Slavia Orthodoxa" (1995), which you can find and download from academia.edu

replies.title: Re: Languages
replies.content: It's why I need to learn Te Reo Maori, because I have a strong belief that some of the old traditions found their way relatively unscathed into Polynesia, and then onto Aotearoa/New Zealand. Some have been written, most haven't, but if one has the key, and some permission, there's a rich mine there. I mean, the Maori (and Polynesian traditions) themselves had an intact inner order tradition with a single God/unmanifest called Io. It's just too damned close to many of the vibratory sounds that provoke mystic states to be a conincidence. All marae (Maori meeting houses) in New Zealand face east, in the original tradition, apart from one marae at the main army base which faces west. The story goes that when asked why, the army chaplain (himself Maori) said that facing west is facing the doors of night, and that the military has the nation's back during those times...

Can we map the tree of life onto the seven planes? What about the golden chain to either? I'm starting to see a pattern. The closest I found is this link and diagram 35.

http://www.the-equinox.org/vol1/no2/eqi02020h.html

Is there a good simple explanation of what the Cabbala is? I'm thinking here along the lines of your simple overview of hermetics philosophy on the hermetix podcast.

Thank you!

replies.title: Re: Tree of Life Seven planes
replies.content:
You can certainly do such a mapping, and that's one way to do it. Regular discursive meditation on the subject is more useful than accepting someone else's mapping, though.

As for a good basic intro to the Cabala, Lon Milo Duquette's book The Chicken Qabalah is probably the best of those in print.

comments.title: eclipse follow up
comments.content:
JMG,

Thank you again for hosting these Q&As. As of this evening the electoral college has voted, and Biden has secured over 270 votes. On the surface things seem to have been rather boring. Do you think your your concern for a clustermuck due to the eclipse related to Barr's resignation, to legal maneuverings still playing out, or some aspect of the government seemingly unrelated to the electoral college? From what I can tell, and I readily admit I am no astrologer, the first state to vote was at around 10am Eastern and the bump over 270 came around 5pm Eastern, while the eclipse was at maximum around 11:13am Eastern. Could there be some "protective effect" of the start times not aligning precisely with the eclipse?

replies.title: Re: eclipse follow up
replies.content: The effects of the eclipse extend over more than five years -- "as many years as there were hours from its beginning to its end" -- and since this one was in a mutable sign its effects will be complex and subtle. The fact that things didn't go splat instantly came as no surprise to me...

comments.title: Etheric absorbers
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

You mentioned many times that water is a good absorber of etheric energy and that linen and silk are good insulators, which is why the traditional lore recommends wrapping ritual implements and talismans in them.

Do you have any go-to sources regarding substances and their etheric properties?

I find it interesting that some modern psychic healing research, apparently independently, has discovered that water and cotton can store whatever healing power the healer produces and transmit it to healees. (Bernard Grad, William Bengston) As far as I could find I didn't see any experiments by them on other substances though.

Grad reported that his healer apparently couldn't heal mice through plastic cages but Bengston somehow managed to get the effect across.

Anyway, I find the idea of scientific research of occult phenomena with controlled variables very interesting. Do you think there is any value in this even if we can't understand the mechanisms and isolate all the variables?

replies.title: Re: Etheric absorbers
replies.content:
1) My book The Encyclopedia of Natural Magic covers much of what's known.

2) One of the most useless superstitions of modern science is the notion that you have to understand the mechanisms before you can start running experiments. If that rule had been followed by Marie Curie we still wouldn't know a thing about radioactivity; it's because she did experiments on an effect with an unknown mechanism that enough information was gathered to figure out the mechanism. I'd be delighted to see more scientific research on occult phenomena; Carl von Reichenbach, the chemist who discovered paraffin, did a bunch of it back in the 19th century.

You were under the wire by eleven minutes, btw, which was quite ample.
